Move played: g0g1 (black to move)
Game: Xiangqi 1981 Best Chen Xiaokun D GM Li Laiqun cc vs sch ppdc | 1981 National Xq Championships
Explain this move.

At this point, Chen had two choices, ||1) C5+4 and 2) H5+6.||C5+4 was relatively more simple and direct. ||H5+6 would be more aggressive but also more treacherous for both sides. There would be no room for error if H5+6 was played. ||After much consideration, Chen decided to play the cannon. ||Please click on the variation below to see what might have happened if Chen played H5+6

Game: Evaluating your Opening: Efficiency Theory 03
Explain this move.

In total,8 moves have been made by red thus far. ||Now, let us calculate the efficiency of both red and black. ||Red has moved: 2 horse moves + 1 chariot moves + 0.5 moves ( by default) = 3.5 moves. The elephant moves are not calculated as red" width="500" height="350" frameborder="0" marginwidth="0" marginheight="0" scrolling="no">   The above is a simple guide to evaluate your opening. But it has its pitfalls too. It does not take into consideration the positioning of the pieces. For example, a well positioned cannon may require the opponent to make a few moves before escaping its threat. Also, it does not take into consideration the value of the pieces. For example, sometimes a chariot is exchanged for a horse and cannon. The chariot might have moved only say x moves but the horse and cannon might have made y moves where y>x. Efficiency is gained by the chariot’s side but the value might not be worth it. The third point to note is better explained with a scenario. Let’s say a pawn has crossed the river and made a few moves. The effective moves for the pawn would be say z moves. However, that initiative is readily erased should the pawn be captured, like in the case of the horse in example 2. So, another important principle in your opening is the ability of your pieces to remain unscathed after making many moves. Note that the above system is generally useful only in openings, ie roughly the first 10-20 moves of the game. Once the game enters mid-game, it becomes useless, as multiple exchanges have already occurred. Positioning of the pieces followed by material advantage becomes key. I was taught this concept when I attended a Xiangqi introductory lesson by GM Hu Ronghua early in the 1990’s when he visited Singapore. The way GM Hu presented the concept opened a whole new world in Xiangqi for me. I still recall GM Hu stressing the importance of trying to make all your moves as 100% (although impossible) efficient as possible. Since then, my buddies and me have coined it the 100% theory. A few months ago, I read about this concept again in an introductory book when I prepared to create this website. It has helped me much and I hope it helps you with your game too. Reference: 1. In Chinese, <<象棋入门>> by 李浭 and 马正福 . With E7+5, black can prevent red from moving R6+5 to block the elephants eye and catching the cannon at the same time.It also has the added benefit of preventing Reds H6+7 and then H7+9 for an attack as black can now defend with E3+1

Move played: i9h9 (black to move)
Game: Always fight to control the center
Explain this move.

The single horse defense was once very popular in the 1950s. If one were to judge the merits of this opening based on the links of the chess pieces and also the balanced nature of it, this counter would score very high. Indeed, this counter was called the The Tree with the Seven Treasures (“七宝连树”).||However, many experts studied it thoroughly and found many ways to exploit the inherent weakness of this opening, causing black to be at a disadvantage most of the time. This is why it is seldom used nowadays as a black counter.||The main problem with this counter is the side horse. It is too far away from the center and would require much effort to develop. This could easily lead black to be even slower in the development of the chess pieces.||Another fallibility is that blacks formation was decided too early and the lack of versatility or inadequate adaptations to reds ensuing attack made it especially vulnerable to precise, targeted attacks by red. This was another reason why this opening has slowly been pushed to the brink of extinction. ||Jim: There has been effort to address this issue by black too. Instead of the filed chariot R9=8, black chose to play R9+1 followed by R9=4, breathing life into this counter. Indeed, when red starts the game with the angels pawn and proceeds to the central cannon variation, blacks thundering defense would often end up with a variant of the single horse defense and it has produced much desirable results. But again, the issue of controlling the center has to be addressed
You are an expert Xiangqi (Chinese Chess) coach. Analyze the position and provide clear, instructive commentary.

But, the moves and variations in that Manual were known practically by everybody so GM Li chose to make this move first. By moving this side horse first, he would keep GM Wang from guessing what attack he would use. ||Often used attacking options for red after this move include: C8=6 (56 cannons), C8=7 (57 cannons) , C8+4 (cross river cannon variation), R9+1 (CC with FR and RR)
